item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Being British and from the 1980s, it made me rather think of Johnny Ball, who did similar programs to Bill Nye (I infer, having honestly never seen one of the latter).

I would also watch the heck out of this, given half a chance.

Made a nice change of pace!

+1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the image comes from here and requires purchase for official use. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would love to see this show. I would gobble up every episode like a little kid with Dora the explorer. Well done! Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I read the footnote that mentioned Wondertainment, I was about to give it a downvote. I strongly dislike the whole gimmick of Wondertainment toys, and most of them I think would be stronger articles without that connection. But in the end, that was the only mention of that GoI, so I don't mind. I mean, it could work as well as the usual mention of sponsors does in real-world educational shows. I never learned a thing about the Helena Rubenstein Foundation except that it donated a bunch of money to all the PBS shows I loved watching as a kid.

One thing that I really loved to see was proper use of [REDACTED] and [DATA EXPUNGED.] Or at least I think it was proper. The expunged portions contained cognitohazards, so it makes total sense for them to be erased from all records, even if the memetic effects were relatively harmless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No experiments are performed in this episode, but SCP-2702-1 gives a tutorial on the proper format for writing an SCP report.

Audibly went PFFFFFFTTTT. What a delight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I was in a place of authority and was able to contact SCP-2702-1, I'd allow him to continue making his show with foundation resorces. It's an easy and entertaining way to educate new staff!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A lot of these comments made the reference to Bill Nye the Science Guy when they described this article, as it was to me when I was linked it. And I can totally see why.

I loved that show, but this article shone on its own. Yeah, I'd totally watch this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like bill nye the science guy, but I would've rather watched this in class than him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sadly, the similarity seems to have been lethal to mine…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bill Nye meets the Foundation.

I'm trying to imagine some kids with a ouija board trying to perform this weird ritual thing they found on tv (with parental supervision of course), while all hell breaks loose at one of the sites.

Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it, but it put me in a bit of a bind, as a I was working on something along similar lines. I'll probably finish writing it up anyway and let the audience decide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In Mexico, we didn't get Bill Nye, but Beakman's World was hugely popular.
I definitely read this SCP in his voice and upvoted.

I only disliked the LOLfoundation episode.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gave this an upvote, what fun it was to read! I love the tie-in with Wondertainment. I can't help but imagine that there's an underworld of anomalous organizations like these, all interacting in significant ways and perhaps to a particular end. Spooky.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked "edutainment with anomalies" well enough, but the episode that includes Prescott before he's been introduced /because this episode is about time travel/ was what got my upvote.

It's still a nostalgia button, but a different one- I flashed back to watching any number of cartoons that broke their own internal consistency, especially when they tried to tell origin stories. (Like, the introductory story for Character A would include a visit to Location B, but then the introductory story for Location B- in a separate episode- would feature Character A, as if expecting the viewer not to notice.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We can't figure out how to contain it, therefore it's Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked it, but i don't really think it should be Keter. It's probably Euclid at best.
Still, nice job, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, they've explicitly shown some of Prescott's coding, and it was mentioned that the Foundation verified that it was legit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If this was made into a Bill Nye remix, I would SO WATCH IT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It clearly stated that no security breach had every occurred at Site-24 resembling the breaches that occurred as well as the guards and researchers were unusually comedic in "the SCP Foundation." This clearly means that it is anomalous, as otherwise the breach would have been recorded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was trying to educate myself on what the top SCPs of 2015 are, and I came across this one, that I hadn't seen before.

This is fantastic. Whole lot of fun.

Also, I see that there were some earlier issues with expungements, but I gotta say, what we have now is probably one of the best examples of expungements done right, today. You know what's going on. In particular:

An experiment involves a ritual designed to summon [REDACTED]. SCP-2702-1 warns viewers that this should not be attempted without parental supervision.

Just my style.

ETA: And where is that picture from? It's fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Silly Anglophones, counting America as two separate continents.

I definitely agree that "abandoned building" is something that's overused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this to be "Alright". The image of one or two serious Foundation researchers sitting down to watch episodes of what's basically an SCP Alton Brown show is hilarious, and the…

[code] No experiments are performed in this episode, but SCP-2702-1 gives a tutorial on the proper format for writing an SCP report.[/code]

… part put it over the edge. Upvote, though I agree with Moose and Scantron. I can hear Alton Brown's goddamned voice in this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We had Mr. Wizard and 3,2,1 Contact, same deal mostly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally imagined Jim Carrey as Professor Abnormal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article clearly stated that Prescott was revealed to be an actual sapient automaton.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From someone who's old enough to have not seen Bill Nye growing up, I still enjoyed it.

Point made from above: excepting the broadcast itself, the show is not outright anomalous. Perhaps some of the methods are or are not. Perhaps does not mean it is. Perhaps this was picked up on a more anomalous broadcast source; maybe this is coming in from near L4, and is a briefing for an alien fleet or…I think the idea is to make it more obvious that the Foundation is the target audience, rather than the general populace. (Most people also no longer get their TV from broadcast anymore. Maybe it pirates a specific syndicated cable channel or HSN.)

OTOH, the tone and flow of it was fantastic. I agree with the expunging completely; well done.

+1 for creative execution

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted this HARD

Why?

Cause It's Bill Nye the motha fuckin' science guy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Chimera - exactly what my headcanon has this as. He's not trying to hurt anyone, but the info *is* dangerous. Kind of like with certain aspects of Wondertainment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't let the older writers get you down. I loved this.

There seems to be a real need from some contributors to make their works "about" something, instead of simply being. This SCP can easily serve as a springboard for any number of Tales, and as it is it (to me) really opens up the world. It's not a grimdark "OMG so scary," but there's a real threat here. The idea that there's a person or group with all of this info, so far beyond the Foundation, keeps things up in the air. Frankly, I loved it. +1'ed with enthusiasm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your work always makes me happy. A Doctor Wondertainment tale involving this would be incredible.

Also, I can totally see this as a Wondertainment project meant to genuinely get kids interested in the subject, a la Wondertainment itself, and just *happens* to be a Keter-class threat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this to be very enjoyable. Maybe it's because I was just reading a selection of the more depressing SCPs, and last night trying to find every haunted/cursed site SCP, but this was one of those balancing pages for me. The existence of light-hearted, humorous, or even somewhat pop-culture referencing SCPs feels important to me because it keeps the dark aspects from toppling and falling on their faces. To address the critique of this skip being a waste of a good idea, or a tired idea, I genuinely felt that the fun and energy of the piece elevate it past it's subject. It was enjoyable to read, and provided an interesting idea about the technology level of the universe, since all groups would have access to the broadcasts and be able to test them.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wish I had the artistic ability to make this fan art!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one on the other hand I loved, because it uses that same frosting on what I at least found to be an inherently great concept, "Bill Nye the Science Guy, but with anomalies." Anything after that is just having fun with the idea of a kid friendly science show all about the SCPverse, which is entertaining in its own right.

That's the thing though — this isn't 'having fun with' the idea, by my standards. It's introducing elements to the idea that would be obligatory for it to survive on the site… it's not terribly imaginative outside of the robot companion, and that doesn't carry it. I've been around long enough and read enough SCPs that it's reasonably clear to me nowadays which parts of an SCP are just sort of there to be there (this is independent of what the author meant — I'm describing function, not intent), and it's most of the SCP here. There may be some gems here — Chimerasame, downthread, gives one — but that doesn't change the fact that there is little to no material here that I would call "having fun with the idea", because I've already had that fun with other articles.

This article derives the vast majority of its appeal from a secondary source (Bill Nye the Science Guy), and it shows, and it's not something I want other articles replicating. If I were trying to establish the wiki's reputation as a respectable creative writing website with someone, I would not say "well they wrote an article that's basically Bill Nye the Science Guy".

Things don't have to be grimdark horror or tear jerkers to forge an emotional connection- done well, straightlaced humor, wonder, coolness for the sake of coolness, quirky characters, and nostalgia (always with a suitably clinical facade, of course) are just as valid ways of connecting to the reader,

Unless I'm misinterpreting you, the fact that you're using this argument is implying that I have an unhealthily limited range of emotions that I'm okay with SCPs eliciting. I don't think I do. I think that banking on nostalgia value is, again, just banking on someone else's creativity to prop up one's own article. If I want Bill Nye nostalgia, I can watch Bill Nye. I think to justify our existence as a creative writing site, we need to do things that other people haven't done already; by extension, any given article on the site needs to do something that isn't just something somebody else did with a new 'frosting'.

Nostalgia is a valid way to connect with the reader, but not in the absence of quality original material.

and some ideas are just inherently interesting or entertaining to explore for their own sake.

And this is fine, except as stated above, this idea is not explored. I come to the site to see people think about or say things about or imagine new ideas, not regurgitate them wholesale onto an article. I don't mean to say that effort was not put into this article, I mean to say that said effort was not placed where it mattered.

You're not wrong for enjoying this, but (OPINION WARNING) I do think it's wrong to say that that alone is sufficient to justify this article's presence on the site. That's how site policy works, for lack of a better system, but it's only viable when we, as readers, go beyond "stays if enjoyable, goes if not" and ask ourselves what is really appropriate for the site.

tl;dr I refuse to settle for nostalgia value when we are capable of doing more and, arguably, must do more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's Bill Nye, but with anomalous stuff.

+1 for BILL, BILL, BILL, BILL

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it — not so much for the simple "Bill Nye, but with anomalies", I'd have left it at no-vote for that concept alone. Rather, for the realization upon reading the test logs that the SCP Foundation itself (or perhaps the SCP Foundation as a representative example of general humanity of this Earth), is in the target audience of the show.

Headcanon: Professor Abnormal is from an alternate reality / the future / an alien planet, and he seeks to educate *civilizations* in much the same way Bill Nye seeks to educate children. The first several logs play out exactly the same way as a Bill Nye show (or Mr. Wizard, etc) with children.

E.g. Bill Nye might assume children have easy access to some sort of implement, but some children watching do not. Professor Abnormal assumes all watchers have access to a _ Analyzer, but some watchers (including us) do not. We carry out a portion of the experiments just as I imagine some children attempt to carry out portions of a Bill Nye-like show.

And time after time the Foundation *actually learns* as a result of this! Not without occasional disastrous effects, but a net positive by and large. This learning is precisely what the Prof intended!

The only thing that doesn't 100% jive with this headcanon is the episode on the SCP Foundation itself.

I don't mind the fact that the item itself may technically not be anomalous. If there is some need that all SCPs are anomalous, then this could be fixed by saying that SCP-2702 is periodically broadcast on a certain TV frequency without any success at locating the source of the broadcast — but honestly I don't see the need for that. It's clear why it has to be contained even though it's not anomalous itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well I'll try something different for my next article then

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does this really need to be anything but what it is?

I liked SCP-2047 well enough to upvote, but mostly because it does the exact same thing you criticize in this one. For me the base idea was, as you so eloquently put it, "so broad and generic and overused that it lacks any real emotional impact." The frosting saved it, however, by building an interesting world and an amusing twist out of a pile of cliches.

This one on the other hand I loved, because it uses that same frosting on what I at least found to be an inherently great concept, "Bill Nye the Science Guy, but with anomalies." Anything after that is just having fun with the idea of a kid friendly science show all about the SCPverse, which is entertaining in its own right.

Things don't have to be grimdark horror or tear jerkers to forge an emotional connection- done well, straightlaced humor, wonder, coolness for the sake of coolness, quirky characters, and nostalgia (always with a suitably clinical facade, of course) are just as valid ways of connecting to the reader, and some ideas are just inherently interesting or entertaining to explore for their own sake. This is what gave us parts of the site like SCP-682; the experiment logs for 212, 261, 294, and 914; and much of the classic site lore… and also something that was forgotten around 2012 in the wake of the site's early growing pains and the turn toward a darker and grittier foundation.

Don't get me wrong; I've enjoyed reading through the "nineties anti-hero" phase of the site over the past year or so since I found the site, and I don't want to see the story driven SCPs, in-universe professionalism, and rounder characterization which that era ushered in go away. Its just that I've also been thrilled to see the site's collective consciousness remembering or relearning a vital part of storytelling over that same year: It always has to be tempered with proper tone and maturity, but it's okay to have something that exists simply because its neato.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted hard. Not because it's awful, but because it's so incredibly disappointing.

You did a fairly good reskin of Bill Nye / similar shows — "Professor Abnormal" is pretty nice, the picture is a great choice, and the transcript with Prescott the robot goes so far as being actively good, but the rest takes a nosedive.

There were some other minor things I enjoyed in here, but I've seen them all in like ten Tanhony SCPs from 2012 (including the structure itself).

Scantron is mostly on point above: this is painting by the numbers. It's most certainly going to survive and be popular based on a competent execution of the concept alone, because of all the nostalgia buttons it's pushing, but it's mostly just really sad.

It's such an incredibly fun concept. You could do so much with this, so much, and you do barely anything at all. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The sentence "Bill Nye the Science Guy, but with anomalies." would have contained just as much information and imagination as this article, except it would have been much faster for me to read. I basically read the whole thing on autopilot once I got past the first sentence of the description, and looking back more carefully now, I don't think I missed out on anything really meaningful. I prefer articles that expand on a concept and say something about it, not just drive it into the ground.

Oh, and "by the way guess what THE APOCALYPSE end of scp" is, honestly, trite. It's just "and then it killz u OOGABOOGA" times about seven billion — it's so broad and generic and overused that it lacks any real emotional impact. If you want to actually end an SCP with mass death or the specter thereof, there has to be some actual buildup to it, either in the article's tone or content. The criminally underappreciated SCP-2047 accomplishes this brilliantly by way of using the latter (prior references to a moonbase, combined with the general plot of the article itself) to generate fridge horror, and by keeping the scope manageable (that is to say, comprehensible — a million is a statistic, etc.).

-1, and I don't think that any sort of expansion or tweaking of the existing material would save that. You need to take your basic concept and go with it in a direction that isn't just painting by the numbers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would watch the hell out of this show. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm just going to say that this is the article that, after several years of lurking, finally prompted me to register an account. Major kudos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, the methods aren't really anomalous, but there is this:

http://www.discovery.com/tv-shows/mayan-doomsday-prophecy/videos/how-the-world-will-end/

item.title: I LOVE THIS
item.content:encoded: I love this article so damn much. ALL OF MY UPVOTES

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
can't stop laughing with the fact that this there is a link between the pic and the scp…
+1
you've done well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly: Your existing way of thinking is being superceeded by an intrusive, viral concept that effects a new, completely foreign, pattern of thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made a small update to address some of the criticisms

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See, I saw reviews declaring this to be an anomalous TV show, and I didn't want to read it. Now that I read it, I see the appeal.

A lot of people dislike this because of similarities to SCP-993, but here's what I have to say: Bobble works because its creepy and not easily understood. The Factory works for the same reasons. This works because it's pretty damn whimsical and not actively harmful, kinda like Wondertainment. People may dislike Wondertainment because it's similar in MO to the Factory, but the important difference is in attitude. Same with this and Bobble (though I don't think of Bobble as being a Factory production).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good stuff. Only problem I had was with the triangulation. Was any attempt made to locate the three places for signal origin? If you put that aspect it in, I'd rather see "empty cornfield" rather than "abandoned building". The latter is a common enough theme in SCP documents that I think it needs to be on the cliche list.

Also, WHAT continent? We've got like 6 of 'em (I count Europe and Aisa as one large continent)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but you didn't completely sell me until the log of the episodes. That he expects kids to easily procure expensive or otherwise impossible equipment helps make this work.

Also, I'm pleased to see gratuitous expungement make a comeback and actually finally work. We've been avoiding it for too long, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one thinking more of Beakman's World then Bill Nye?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good lord.
Yes.

YES

+1 for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure how I feel about this, overall. Just glad there wasn't an immediate jump to "All About 682" or such.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Forced to disagree, after checking to make sure. The way you think already exists (unless you don't think at all, and in that case be sure to mop up the drool); it's being changed to a different "way".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Doctor Wondertainment is listed as one of the program's sponsors

Grumble grumble now I need to write that tale…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how clueless this shows the Foundation to be, in that they are learning a massive amount from an what is apparently a children's show with oversimplified explanations to everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love seeing this finally up on the main site.

I just had a great mental scene pop into my head of an AWCY? artist watching this show stoned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think either works for a memetic effect, really…its implanting a mind altering unit of code, that generates a pattern of thought or behavior.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read this entirely in Bill Nye's voice. So consider the following;

Win. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bill Nye the Science Guy meets Bobble the Clown? Honestly, if you hadn't totally nailed the Bill Nye parts, I woulda downvoted for being pointlessly Keter and for being just another anomalous broadcast. But you've managed to push all the right buttons and make an actual interesting personality for the guy, so neutral voted on my part.
